title: NOC 94212 – Plastic products assemblers, finishers and inspectors | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s plastics industry is undergoing a high-tech revolution centered on sustainable polymers and advanced manufacturing. Plastic Products Assemblers, Finishers, and Inspectors (NOC 94212) are the technical experts who assemble and refine a vast array of essential goods—from high-precision medical components and aerospace parts to eco-friendly consumer packaging. As the industry shifts toward "Circular Economy" models and automated production, international workers with experience in ultrasonic welding, digital quality control, and robotic assembly are seeing a significant surge in demand, particularly in Ontario’s "Industrial Corridor."

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Occupations usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group942 – Assemblers and inspectors in manufacturing

duties: Assembly: Operating specialized equipment to bond plastic parts together using adhesives, solvents, or ultrasonic welding techniques.
Finishing: Trimming, sanding, and polishing plastic parts to remove imperfections and achieve precise dimensional specifications.
Inspection: Examining finished plastic products for defects, such as cracks, surface blemishes, or air bubbles, using both visual checks and digital gauges.
Machine Tending: Monitoring automated assembly lines and clearing machine blockages to ensure continuous production.
2026 Update: Utilizing digital tablets to log "Carbon-Neutral" production data and monitoring automated quality sensors via IoT-connected production dashboards.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Training: Several weeks of on-the-job training are typically provided by the employer.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer in a plastic manufacturing plant is often required for advancement to assembler or inspector roles.
Manual Dexterity: Strong hand-eye coordination and the ability to work with precision components.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a "Balanced" market with localized shortages in medical and high-tech manufacturing hubs. The complexity of 2026-grade medical-grade plastics requires a new generation of Inspectors with digital troubleshooting skills. Over 3,200 openings are projected due to the modernization of Canada's domestic plastic manufacturing sector.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is driven by the expansion of the medical device and sustainable packaging sectors:

Ontario (GTA & Southwestern Ontario): Very High demand. This region is the hub of Canada’s plastic manufacturing, currently facing a technical labor shortage in the automotive and medical supply sectors.
Quebec (Montreal & Montérégie): Strong demand. Focused on high-precision plastic assembly for the aerospace and pharmaceutical industries.
Alberta: Good prospects. Driven by the manufacturing of industrial plastic components for the energy and agricultural sectors.
British Columbia: Stable demand. Focused on specialized high-end consumer products and sustainable marine-grade plastics.
National Trend: Approximately 13,500 people work in this occupation. The market favors those who can bridge the gap between mechanical assembly and digital inspection.
Why Work in Canada as a Plastic Products Assembler?
Sustainable Innovation: Work with 2026-grade bio-plastics, recycled polymers, and the latest eco-friendly additive manufacturing materials.
High-Tech Environment: Gain experience with advanced technologies, including ultrasonic welders, robotic pick-and-place arms, and automated finishing lines.
Path to PR: Frequently prioritized in Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) streams and is a core qualifier for the 2026 TR to PR transition programs.
Industry Diversity: Your skills are transferable across sectors, including healthcare, automotive, aerospace, and consumer electronics.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Plastic product assemblers are classified as "Essential Manufacturing Workers," making them high-priority candidates for this 33,000-person transition quota.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s industrial corridor.
OINP Express Entry Human Capital Priorities: Periodically targets candidates with experience in specialized manufacturing and medical supply sectors.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
